data.title: Live updates: DHS shutdown inches closer; Trump visits with troops in North Carolina
data.description:
The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) is gearing up for a shutdown after Senate Democrats on Thursday blocked bills that would keep the lights on.

Senators left Washington without a deal, showing just how far apart Democrats and White House negotiators are on a potential agreement to reform Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) after two fatal shootings in Minneapolis heightened scrutiny of DHS.

Starting at midnight Saturday, key agencies overseen by the department — including the Transportation Security Administration, Federal Emergency Management Agency, U.S. Coast Guard and U.S. Secret Service, among others — will see a lapse in funding.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) and Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (N.Y.) told their colleagues to be prepared to return to the Capitol on short notice if an agreement is reached.

President Trump, ahead of his trip to North Carolina, told reporters that he is still open to negotiating with Democrats on the issue.

The president is visiting Fort Bragg on Friday to speak with military families and the specialized troops that assisted with the ouster of embattled Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro earlier this year.

Trump will then travel to his Mar-a-Lago resort in Palm Beach, Fla., for the weekend.

Follow along live below.

data.title: Former UK ambassador Mandelson summoned to testify before Congress over Epstein links
data.description:
A former UK ambassador has been summoned to testify before Congress over his ties to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. 

Two members of the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform requested testimony from Peter Mandelson in a Friday email to the former British official as part of the panel’s investigation into Epstein’s activities. 

Ranking Member Robert Garcia (D-Calif.) and Rep. Suhas Subramanyam (D-Va.) said that the Justice Department’s files related to its Epstein probe indicated that Mandelson “may have knowledge of his activities related to our investigation.”

“In the interest of justice for the victims of Jeffrey Epstein, we request that you cooperate with the Committee’s investigation by sitting for a transcribed interview with the Committee,” the lawmakers wrote.

Mandelson stepped down from his role as British ambassador to the U.S. after links to Epstein became public. He previously served as Britain’s member of the EU Commission and was a member of Parliament. 

Garcia and Subramanyam pointed to “evidence … demonstrating your close ties” to Epstein in their Friday letter, including a handwritten note in the sex offender’s 50th Birthday Book and financial transfers from Epstein. Garcia and Subramanyam also referenced an image included in the Epstein files that “appears to depict you in your underwear alongside an
unidentified woman.”

“While you no longer serve as British Ambassador to the United States and have stepped down from the House of Lords, it is clear that you possessed extensive social and business ties to Jeffrey Epstein and hold critical information pertaining to our investigation of Epstein’s operations,” the lawmakers wrote. “Given the appalling allegations regarding Epstein’s conduct, we request that you make yourself available for a transcribed interview with Committee staff regarding the crimes of Jeffrey Epstein and his co-conspirators.”

Garcia and Subramanyan mandated a response from Mandelson by Feb. 27.

The letter comes amid the Oversight Committee’s continued investigation into Epstein. The committee has subpoenaed former President Bill Clinton and former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton to provide testimony in the investigation. The Clintons are set to appear before the committee at the end of February.

data.title: At 30, E-Rate is a proven success. Let's keep improving it.
data.description:
Thirty years ago, Congress passed the Telecommunications Act of 1996 based on a straightforward principle: The benefits of modern communications networks should reach every community, not just the well-resourced ones.

Back then, the “world wide web” was growing and increasingly necessary for daily life, but fewer than a quarter of U.S. adults had internet access. To address that gap efficiently, Congress created E-Rate, a discount matching program that enabled public libraries and schools across the country to obtain high-speed internet access at significantly reduced cost.

As the program hits its 30th anniversary this month, we should celebrate E-Rate for what it is: a quiet powerhouse that has given nearly every American access to high-speed internet through public libraries and schools.

For public libraries, the impact of E-Rate has a human face: a job seeker applying for a new position; workers filing taxes on time; a family accessing student homework portals, remote learning and telehealth appointments; and communities using library technology to keep in touch with loved ones during natural disasters. In many towns and neighborhoods, the public library remains the only reliable place to get broadband speeds that make modern life possible. When home connectivity is unavailable, unreliable or unaffordable, the library is where people come to get online.

The internet also powers the practical, behind-the-scenes functions that keep libraries running. The full range of library services requires high-speed internet: building and managing collections, checking materials in and out, supporting research, and helping patrons find credible information efficiently. Today’s card catalogs do not work without internet access.

Beyond powering countless library services, the E-Rate program provides broadband that entire communities rely on. Six tribal libraries and two schools in New Mexico, for example, leveraged their access to E-Rate funding and built two tribally owned and operated modern fiber networks. Covering 120 miles and six sovereign nations, the networks dramatically increased internet access speeds and decreased costs, and it would not have been built without federal E-Rate funding.

Not only has E-Rate made broadband more accessible for communities over its 30-year history, it is now considered a model for other federal programs in terms of oversight for federal awards and grants. Of five programs reviewed by the Government Accountability Office in 2025 for compliance with requirements for preventing waste, fraud and abuse, E-Rate was the only one to receive a perfect score.

Despite the success of E-Rate in filling connectivity gaps over the years, the program is not perfect. Only about half of public libraries participate in E-Rate. The complexity of the application process continues to be a key barrier. Many libraries, especially small, rural and tribal, operate with limited administrative capacity. When a single person is the director, technology lead and grant writer, complicated forms, procurement requirements and compliance rules can turn a valuable benefit into an unrealistic burden. For a large system with specialized staff, complexity is inconvenient. For a small library, it can be disqualifying.

Over the last 30 years, ALA has continually worked with the FCC to improve the program, remove barriers, and increase access to this vital program for library applicants. The FCC can simplify applications and procurement steps and preserve E-Rate’s already strong program integrity safeguards. These streamlining measures mean less time spent navigating paperwork and more time spent delivering public value, and more libraries across the country with highspeed broadband.

Congress, which has long recognized that E-Rate is as important today as it was in 1996, has its own part to play in ensuring the program remains a critical part of the Universal Service Fund. Today, a bipartisan, bicameral working group is considering reforms to ensure the Universal Service Fund, including the E-Rate discount, continues well into the future. As Congress moves toward contribution reform, they should prioritize reliable funding that is not dependent on annual appropriations.

Congress should also keep its eye on the courts. The Supreme Court upheld the constitutionality of the Universal Service Fund in 2025, but recent renewed attacks claim that E-Rate specifically is unconstitutional. Threats to a program with such results — and fiscal integrity that exceeds many government programs — are short-sighted and jeopardize efforts to bridge the digital divide for the nearly one in ten Americans that have no high-speed internet access.

Anniversaries are a time to take stock. Thirty years after the Telecommunications Act of 1996, the country is more connected, but many people still rely on public institutions to reach the digital world. E-Rate is one of the most effective, accountable tools the nation has for making sure that geography and income do not determine who can learn, work and participate in our connected world. We should celebrate E-Rate and — until broadband is universally accessible and affordable — strengthen it.

Sam Helmick is president of the American Library Association and serves as community and access services coordinator for Iowa City Public Library.

data.title: Rhode Island State House rug that cost $70K gets state seal wrong
data.description:
PROVIDENCE, R.I. (WPRI) — Officials are blaming “human error” after a new rug at the Rhode Island State House somehow got the seal of approval despite having the wrong seal.

The $70,000 rug was installed Feb. 2 in the second-floor State Room, which is located next to the governor’s office and is often the setting for news conferences and ceremonial events, with Gilbert Stuart’s historic portrait of George Washington hanging on the wall.

But Nexstar’s WPRI discovered that the 1,125-square-foot floor covering has the wrong state seal stitched into it, not once but twice. Both bear the words: “Seal of Rhode Island and Providence.” Voters removed the phrase “and Providence Plantations” from the state’s official name in 2020.

The two inaccurate seals are currently being covered up by round area rugs, which still have their $110 price tags from Ocean State Job Lot on the bottom.

Karen Greco, a spokesperson for the R.I. Department of Administration, acknowledged the mishap in an email.

“The mistake on the seal was simply human error, and the plan is to correct it,” she told WPRI. She did not say what office was responsible.

It’s unclear how much more money taxpayers will have to spend fixing the seals. The rug was purchased from Stark Carpet Corp. of Boston.

“The two seals will be hand-cut out of the rug and new seals will be sewn in,” Greco said. “We are waiting for a timeline and a cost estimate for the correction.”

Stark Carpet has not responded to a request for comment.

Replacing the rug was one of many items state officials put on their to-do list after the 2020 vote to remove the “Plantations” phrase from the end of the state’s formal name, requiring a change to the state seal.

The McKee administration has been making slow progress since then, removing the language from various other locations at the State House, though some are harder to address than others.

data.title: Roughly half of Republicans disapprove of Trump Greenland push: Survey
data.description:
Nearly half of Republicans disapprove of President Trump’s ambitions to acquire Greenland, according to a new survey. 

The poll, released by The Associated Press-NORC Center for Public Affairs Research on Friday, found that 51 percent of GOP voters approve of how the president has handled the Danish territory. Forty-eight percent of Republican respondents disapprove.

Among Democratic and independent voters, the topic is much less popular. Only 15 percent of independents and a mere 7 percent of Democrats are in favor of Trump’s handling of Greenland. And overall, 24 percent of respondents approve.

Trump has been outspoken about his plans to acquire Greenland, citing national security threats from Russia and China. 

The president has even suggested a military takeover of the Arctic island and said he would impose tariffs on European allies who opposed the acquisition — threats he later backed off after a meeting with NATO Chief Mark Rutte. At the time, Trump and Rutte announced the framework of a deal on the future of Greenland, but negotiations are ongoing.

Lawmakers from both sides of the aisle have critiqued Trump’s moves, with some Republicans promising to intervene if the president attempted to seize Greenland by force. A bipartisan group of legislators traveled to Copenhagen in January to meet with Danish officials about the threats.

The U.S. already enjoys broad military access to the territory through a Cold War agreement, which offers the U.S. military “free access” to operate in the country’s defense areas. The treaty was updated in 2004 to provide broader rights due to changes to Danish military bases and evolving North Atlantic security concerns.  

The AP-NORC poll was conducted Feb. 5-8 among 1,156 U.S. adults with a margin of error of 3.9 percentage points. The margin of error for Republican respondents is 6.1 points.

data.title: Musk labels Anthropic as 'misanthropic and evil'
data.description:
Tech billionaire Elon Musk slammed Anthropic on Thursday as “misanthropic and evil,” after the AI firm announced its latest round of funding.

Musk, who owns a competing AI company xAI, alleged that Anthropic’s AI “hates Whites & Asians, especially Chinese, heterosexuals and men.” He did not point to any specific examples.

“This is misanthropic and evil,” he added. “Fix it. Frankly, I don’t think there is anything you can do to escape the inevitable irony of Anthropic ending up being Misanthropic. You were doomed to this fate when you chose your name.”

His comments came in response to Anthropic’s announcement Thursday that it had raised $30 billion in funding at a valuation of $380 billion. The Hill has reached out to Anthropic for comment.

Musk has previously taken aim at his AI rivals, frequently feuding with OpenAI CEO Sam Altman. The tech mogul, who helped found OpenAI, sued Altman and the company in 2024, accusing them of abandoning the firm’s founding values and manipulating him into investing in the ChatGPT maker.

He left OpenAI’s board in 2018 and founded his own AI firm xAI in 2023. xAI is currently in the midst of a transition, after Musk announced last week that it was merging with SpaceX. Two xAI co-founders and several employees departed the company this week, with Musk noting he had “reorganized” the firm in recent days.

Anthropic, which was founded with a focus on AI safety, has at times found itself at odds with members of the Trump administration and its allies in Silicon Valley. The White House has pushed for light-touch regulation on AI, opposing state efforts to regulate the technology.

Meanwhile, the AI firm behind Claude has backed some state AI regulations, most notably a California bill requiring major AI companies disclose safety information. The measure was signed into law by California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) in September.

Shortly after, White House AI and crypto czar David Sacks accused the company of being “woke.” Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei responded at the time that “Anthropic, the administration, and leaders across the political spectrum want the same thing.”

As AI-related groups ramp up their spending on the upcoming midterm elections, Anthropic announced Thursday that it was giving $20 million to Public First Action, a group launched last year to support efforts develop AI safeguards.

It separately announced Friday that Chris Liddell, who served as deputy White House chief of staff in Trump’s first administration, was joining its board of directors. Liddell was also previously the chief financial officer of Microsoft and General Motors.

“Chris has spent his career at the intersection of technology, public service, and governance—and has a track record of helping organizations get those things right when the stakes are highest,” Anthropic co-founder and president Daniela Amodei said in a statement.

“As AI’s impact on society grows, that kind of judgement and experience is exactly what we seek on our board,” she continued.

data.title: The US is pushing back on Europe's big cheese gatekeeping
data.description:
For the first time in years, the U.S. is going on the offensive against the expansion of Europe’s geographical indications — think brie, gouda and parmesan.

Those uncapitalized cheese names aren’t typos. They are listed that way on purpose in several recent U.S. trade deals as a shot across Europe’s bow.

It is a transatlantic trade tension that’s playing out in third markets. Although American cheese is consistently ranked among the world’s finest, the European Union insists that widely produced types like brie, gouda and parmesan trade unfairly on the reputation of their European namesakes — the region of Brie (France) and the cities of Parma (Italy) and Gouda (the Netherlands). 

Since roughly 2015, Brussels has embedded this claim in its trade agreements, obligating partners to protect cheeses whose “quality, reputation, or other characteristic[s]” are said to derive from their place of origin. That is geographical indication enforcement. 

After the collapse of the World Trade Organization’s Doha round, Brussels leveraged developing countries’ demand for agricultural market access to propagate its geographical indication regime. John Clarke, the EU’s former chief agricultural negotiator and architect of the EU-China geographical indication agreement, acknowledged that geographical indications were implicitly “the currency of exchange” for the agricultural concessions offered to Central America, the Andean Community and South Africa.  

The strategy worked, resulting in a thick web of geographical indication agreements that treat European naming conventions as global rules. 

Each EU geographical indication chapter follows the same pattern: a lengthy annex listing hundreds of protected products, coupled with two provisions that U.S. policymakers view as corrosive. The first mandates the phase-out of competing trademarks, forcing firms that lawfully acquired rights in good faith to surrender them if a European geographical indication has a similar name.

The second goes further still, banning any term listed as a geographical indication from being treated as a generic — no matter how consumers use or understand it, even when the term functions globally as a style rather than a place.

This approach sits uneasily with World Trade Organization law, which explicitly preserves members’ ability to treat terms as generic. In practice, the EU rules raise compliance costs, restrict competition and freeze comparative advantage by regulation.

Until recently, the U.S. was on the defensive. In 2024, when Chile signed an interim trade agreement with the EU protecting 234 geographical indications, the Biden administration responded by negotiating an exchange of letters under the U.S.-Chile agreement to preserve protection for U.S. generic meats and cheeses. It was a necessary move, but hardly a strategy. 

That has now changed. In August, the Office of the U.S. Trade Representative directly challenged Brussels in its Special 301 Report, warning that the U.S. would counter any EU geographical indication initiatives that “undercut market access” across more than 20 countries. Since then, Washington has signed a dozen bilateral frameworks to negotiate future agreements. The three deals completed so far — with Cambodia, Malaysia and El Salvador — have begun to turn rhetoric into action. 

All three agreements include “meat and cheese” provisions listing generic terms both parties agree to protect. Compared to the EU-China geographical indication deal, which safeguards 33 cheeses, the U.S. lists cover roughly 58 percent, leaving behind obscure names like “Kefalograviera” while preserving everyday names that matter commercially.

The differences among the deals are just as revealing. In the U.S.-Cambodia agreement, Article 2.3 allows the U.S. to use geographical indication terms that Cambodia otherwise recognizes where there is “no given quality, reputation, or other characteristic of the good that is essentially attributable to its geographical origin.” This is a direct rejection of the EU’s expansive definition of geographical indications.

In the U.S.-Malaysia agreement, the approach is even more assertive. A footnote to Article 2.4 provides that Malaysia “shall not protect or recognize a predetermined list of geographical indications.” This isn’t a carve-out; it’s a preemptive strike.

The timing is not an accident. The EU is actively negotiating its own trade agreement with Malaysia, with draft language that would permanently bar listed terms from ever becoming generic.

Brussels, however, is widening the battle. In December, the EU took the unprecedented step of extending geographical indication protection to “craft and industrial products,” exporting the model beyond food and agriculture into manufacturing, design and artisanal goods. That expansion would trap American small and medium-sized firms in the EU’s geographical indication web.

It is unclear whether Washington can reverse more than a decade of EU norm-setting on geographical indications. But for the first time in years, the U.S. is no longer merely reacting. And if the administration succeeds, it may finally turn Europe’s geographical indication regime into swiss — not “Suisse” — cheese.  

Marc L. Busch is the Karl F. Landegger Professor of International Business Diplomacy at the Walsh School of Foreign Service, Georgetown University. Ben Whitfield is a junior in the Walsh School of Foreign Service, Georgetown University.

data.title: Bessent expects Senate Republicans to proceed with Fed chief nomination hearing
data.description:
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said he has reached an “agreement” with the Senate Banking Committee and expects them to proceed with a hearing to nominate the next Federal Reserve chair after a two-week standstill.

Sen. Thom Tillis, a senior member of the Senate Banking Committee, had blocked the holding of a hearing to consider Kevin Warsh’s nomination. President Trump announced Warnick as his pick to serve as the next chair of the Central Bank on Jan. 30.

Tillis said he would stop all of the president’s nominations while a criminal investigation into Fed Chair Jerome Powell remains ongoing. 

Bessent said on CNBC that he talked with a Senate Republican working group on Tuesday and, from that conversation, expects them to move forward with the hearing.  

“It’s important to get the hearings underway, and I think we have an agreement to do that,” Bessent said on CNBC’s “Squawk Box” Friday morning. 

He added, “my understanding is that we are going to proceed with the hearing.”

Tillis responded to Bessent’s comments saying that he does not intend to back down from his promise.

“I have no intention of allowing any Fed board nominee to move forward out of committee and to be confirmed, until this matter is settled,” he said on Bloomberg TV Friday following Bessent’s appearance on CNBC. “We could have a hearing all we want, but until the investigation is done, I still believe that the initial inquiry and the investigation was a flex to try and get the current chair to step aside.”

In an effort to move forward with the hearing, Bessent offered to move the probe into Powell from the Department of Justice to the Banking Committee. Tillis welcomed the additional oversight on the probe but maintained that he would continue to block Warsh from proceeding. 

“It doesn’t change my posture,” he said.

The DOJ is investigating Powell for the cost of his proposed plans to renovate one of the central bank’s buildings, which had risen to $2.5 billion. But lawmakers speculate the probe has more to do with Trump’s desire to oust Powell, whose term will end in May, over his decision to not back additional interest rate cuts.

Tillis and others have seen these actions as a threat to the independence of the central bank.

“We could have a hearing all we want, but until the investigation is done, I still believe that the initial inquiry and the investigation was a flex to try and get the current chair to step aside,” Tillis said on Bloomberg TV Friday. 

“This is foundational to Fed independence, and I, for one, am going to stand on the side of certainty, and Fed independence is what delivers certainty in our markets,” he added.

data.title: Trump finally ends Minnesota ICE op after two Americans killed
data.description:
The Trump administration says it’s ending “Operation Metro Surge” in Minnesota after two months of aggressive immigration enforcement that sparked protests and, tragically, left two U.S. citizens, Renee Good and Alex Pretti, dead.  

Border czar Tom Homan made the announcement yesterday: “I have proposed and President Trump has concurred that this surge operation conclude. A significant draw down has already been underway this week and will continue to the next week.” 

Homan says the surge is ending because cooperation between federal, state and local law enforcement has improved, meaning more immigration detentions will now happen inside jails rather than through street operations. He also says local agencies are responding more quickly and consistently to protests.  

Gov. Tim Walz acknowledged the fear won’t disappear overnight. Writing on X, “The long road to recovery starts now. The impact on our economy, our schools, and people’s lives won’t be reversed overnight. That work starts today.”  

At a press conference, Walz emphasized that Minnesota has always supported targeted enforcement and that nothing fundamentally changed in the state’s approach.  

And that’s where this gets complicated.  

For months, the Trump administration has framed this crackdown as a mission to deport the “worst of the worst.” But according to an internal Department of Homeland Security document obtained by CBS News, ICE made roughly 393,000 arrests across the country between last year and this year; less than 14 percent of those arrested had charges or convictions for violent crimes like homicide, robbery, sexual assault, kidnapping or arson.  

At the same time, ICE official Marcos Charles suggested detainers sent to “the state’s jails and prisons” weren’t being honored — but then later acknowledged the Minnesota Department of Corrections had fully cooperated. DHS has said there are about 1,360 people with ICE detainers in Minnesota prisons and jails. The Minnesota Department of Corrections says the number is closer to 300. Minnesota Department of Corrections Commissioner Paul Schnell pushed back forcefully, saying that ICE officials “continue to publicly repeat information that is inaccurate and misleading. This is no longer simple misunderstanding.”  

Since Dec. 1, the state has turned over 14 people with ICE detainers after they completed their sentences. It’s important to note, before an inmate can be turned over to ICE, they must complete their prison sentence.  

So here’s my question: If Minnesota was honoring detainers, if the dispute was about data and communication, why did it take a massive federal surge, widespread fear, and two American lives lost to reach this middle ground?  

Republicans argue sanctuary policies tie the hands of law enforcement. Democrats argue the sweeps were overly aggressive and lacked accountability. Both sides have talking points. But governance isn’t supposed to be about proving a point — it’s supposed to be about solving a problem.  

Politics is hard. Immigration is hard. But when cooperation was possible all along, and when the facts were this murky, escalation was not inevitable. It was a choice. And the cost of that choice is something Minnesota is now left to live with. 

Lindsey Granger is a NewsNation contributor and co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising.” This column is an edited transcription of her on-air commentary.

data.title: Graham: 'Who gives a s— who owns Greenland? I don't'
data.description:
Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.), who is closely allied with President Trump, distanced himself from the Trump administration’s maneuvering to seize control of Greenland, declaring in an interview: “Who gives as s— who owns Greenland?”

Graham downplayed the significance of the Trump administration’s aggressive posturing to take over the mineral-rich territory, which belongs to Denmark, a bid for territorial expansion that has stressed relationships with NATO allies.

“Here’s what I tell my European friends: Greenland is behind us but the goal is to get outcomes. Who gives a s— who owns Greenland? I don’t,” Graham told Politico’s Jonathan Martin during an interview on the sidelines of the Munich Security Conference.

Graham said the United States’ longstanding European allies should stop worrying about Trump seizing Denmark’s sovereign territory, arguing Trump has achieved the assurances he needs to build up a major U.S. military presence on the vast island.

“If you’re nervous, have a beer, go see a doctor. Stop being nervous,” he quipped. “The point is everybody loves NATO. I love it more because people are doing more,” referring to increased defense spending by NATO’s European members.

“How many of you believe NATO would be contributing this much money if it weren’t for Trump?” Graham asked. “He’s giving them a reason to do it and they’re doing it so let’s celebrate.”

He argued that Europe and the United States have collaborated to make Ukraine’s army “the strongest army in Europe,” asserting “it’s not even close.”

He said Trump will build out defenses in Greenland to deter Russian and Chinese expansion into the Europe.

“The point is Greenland is going to be more fortified because Donald Trump, once he feels like it’s his brand or he has some buy-in, is going to go big,” he said.  

Graham is leading a congressional delegation trip to the Munich Security Conference this weekend with Sen. Sheldon Whitehouse (D-R.I.).

Sens. Lisa Murkowski (R-Alaska), Mark Warner (D-Va.), Chris Coons (D-Del.), Richard Blumenthal (D-Conn.) and Steve Daines (R-Mont.) are some of the senators participating in the trip.
